coredata.dc:title: Enhanced structural homogeneity and conductivity in (Nb0.32V0.17Mo0.17W0.17Co0.17)2O4 high-entropy oxide electrodes via graphene quantum dot integration and super-exchange interaction for high-performance supercapacitors

coredata.dc:description: Structural inhomogeneity and poor conductivity hinder the practical application of high-entropy oxide (HEO) in high-performance supercapacitors. Herein, we present a synergistic strategy involving boron-doped, histidine/serine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (BHS-GQD) and super-exchange interactions to address these challenges in (Nb0.32V0.17Mo0.17W0.17Co0.17)2O4 HEO system. The strong coordination of BHS-GQD with metal ions enables atomic-level uniformity of metal distribution in both solution and solid states, facilitating the formation of long-range ordered HEO nanocrystal with interconnected ion transport channels. Remarkably, BHS-GQD incorporation enhances electrical conductivity by over 45-fold, Li⁺ diffusion coefficient by 1793-fold, and electrochemically active sites by 120-fold. Additionally, the engineered nine-coordinated Mn+-O2—Co2+ super-exchange units significantly boost hybrid capacitive deionization. The optimized HEO-BHS-GQD composite achieves an exceptional specific capacitance of 2415.7 F g−1, surpassing the pristine HEO by 8-fold. A symmetric supercapacitor fabricated with HEO-BHS-GQD electrodes delivers outstanding performance: high capacitance (340 F g−1 at 1 A g−1), superior rate capability (161.3 F g−1at 50 A g−1), exceptional cycling stability (96.8 % retention after 10,000 cycles), and high energy density (153 W h kg−1 at 900 W kg−1). This work provides a novel paradigm for tailoring structural homogeneity and charge transport in HEO through rational interfacial engineering and electronic structure modulation.

head.ce:title: Enhanced structural homogeneity and conductivity in (Nb0.32V0.17Mo0.17W0.17Co0.17)2O4 high-entropy oxide electrodes via graphene quantum dot integration and super-exchange interaction for high-performance supercapacitors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Structural inhomogeneity and poor conductivity hinder the practical application of high-entropy oxide (HEO) in high-performance supercapacitors. Herein, we present a synergistic strategy involving boron-doped, histidine/serine-functionalized graphene quantum dot (BHS-GQD) and super-exchange interactions to address these challenges in (Nb0.32V0.17Mo0.17W0.17Co0.17)2O4 HEO system. The strong coordination of BHS-GQD with metal ions enables atomic-level uniformity of metal distribution in both solution and solid states, facilitating the formation of long-range ordered HEO nanocrystal with interconnected ion transport channels. Remarkably, BHS-GQD incorporation enhances electrical conductivity by over 45-fold, Li⁺ diffusion coefficient by 1793-fold, and electrochemically active sites by 120-fold. Additionally, the engineered nine-coordinated Mn+-O2—Co2+ super-exchange units significantly boost hybrid capacitive deionization. The optimized HEO-BHS-GQD composite achieves an exceptional specific capacitance of 2415.7 F g−1, surpassing the pristine HEO by 8-fold. A symmetric supercapacitor fabricated with HEO-BHS-GQD electrodes delivers outstanding performance: high capacitance (340 F g−1 at 1 A g−1), superior rate capability (161.3 F g−1at 50 A g−1), exceptional cycling stability (96.8 % retention after 10,000 cycles), and high energy density (153 W h kg−1 at 900 W kg−1). This work provides a novel paradigm for tailoring structural homogeneity and charge transport in HEO through rational interfacial engineering and electronic structure modulation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: We firstly reported one strategy for significant improvement of structural homogeneity and conductivity in (Nb0.32V0.17Mo0.17W0.17Co0.17)2O4 high entropy oxide as a desirable electrode material for high performance supercapacitors by introducing graphene quantum dot and superexchange interaction.

ce:section.ce:para: Global industrialization and rising living standards escalate energy demands, yet fossil fuel reliance exacerbates environmental crises [1,2]. While renewable energy systems offer sustainable alternatives, their intermittency and geographical constraints necessitate efficient energy storage [3]. Supercapacitors, distinguished by high power density and cyclability, remain constrained by inferior energy density compared to batteries [4]. Compared with carbon materials [5], transition metal oxides leverage multi-electron redox reactions for enhanced capacitance but suffer from intrinsic poor conductivity and structural degradation [6,7].

High-entropy oxide (HEO), as a novel class of multi-cation material stabilized by configurational entropy, have garnered significant attention for their synergistic enhancement of electronic conductivity and structural stability in energy storage applications [8–10]. However, conventional synthesis methods for HEO nanoparticles-including carbothermal shock [11], selective phase dissolution [12], thermal plasma [13], and sol-gel techniques [14] face persistent challenges. For instance, the carbothermal shock method, while rapid, demands extreme temperatures (>2000 K) and ultrahigh cooling rates (>100 K s−1) to suppress metal segregation, alongside costly metalloorganic precursors [15]. Selective phase dissolution routes involve multi-step dealloying and oxidation processes that generate substantial wastewater [16], whereas thermal plasma methods struggle with nanostructure controllability [17]. Sol-gel approaches, though widely adopted, often fail to maintain metal dispersion during annealing due to weakened coordination interactions between surfactant and metal ion [18–20]. Collectively, these limitations hinder the practical deployment of HEOs in high-performance supercapacitors, particularly due to: (1) synthesis complexities and phase inhomogeneity, (2) ambiguous electrochemical mechanisms in multi-component systems, (3) sluggish charge transport kinetics, (4) cycling instability from lattice strain, and (5) scalability barriers tied to noble metal usage and process costs.

In this work, we address these challenges through a dual-functional modification strategy for the (Nb0.32V0.17Mo0.17W0.17Co0.17)2O4 HEO system. By incorporating boron-doped, histidine/serine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (BHS-GQDs) and engineering nine-coordinated Mn+-O2--Co2+ super-exchange networks, we achieve atomic-level metal homogenization and enhanced charge transfer kinetics. The resultant HEO-BHS-GQD composite exhibits long-range crystallographic order with interconnected ion channels and oxygen vacancy modulation, enabling exceptional supercapacitor performance for wearable electronics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of boron-doped histidine/serine-functionalized graphene quantum dots (BHS-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: BHS-GQDs were synthesized via a modified hydrothermal method [21]. Briefly, citric acid (19.2 g), boric acid (H₃BO₃, 1.2 g), L-histidine (15.5 g), and L-serine (2.1 g) were dissolved in 50 mL ultrapure water. The solution was transferred into a 200 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave, heated at 175 °C for 6 h, cooled to ambient temperature, and freeze-dried to yield the final product. Control samples (GQD and HS-GQD) were prepared by omitting H₃BO₃ or both H₃BO₃ and amino acids, respectively.

A precursor solution was prepared by dissolving BHS-GQD (1 g) and H₂O₂ (10 mL, 30 wt%) in 60 mL ultrapure water. Niobium oxalate (0.54 g), ammonium metavanadate (0.06 g), ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate (0.09 g), cobalt chloride hexahydrate (0.13 g), and sodium tungstate dihydrate (0.18 g) were sequentially added under vigorous stirring until a homogeneous solution formed. The mixture was impregnated into 1 g cotton fibers, vacuum-dried at 60 °C for 8 h, and annealed under N₂ at 850 °C (5 °C min−1 ramp) for 3 h to obtain HEO-BHS-GQD. Variants (e.g., Nb₂O₄-BHS-GQD) were synthesized by adjusting metal salt stoichiometry, while control samples (HEO, HEO-GQD) were prepared by omitting or substituting BHS-GQD.

Morphological and structural analyses were performed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL S-4800) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100). Crystallographic properties were investigated via X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 ADVANCE, Cu Kα, λ = 0.15406 nm). Chemical states were analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos Axis Supra, Al Kα), while oxygen vacancies were quantified via electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR, Bruker EMX PLUS, X-band, 9.8 GHz). Additional characterization included Raman spectroscopy (Renishaw InVia, 532 nm laser), UV–vis diffuse reflectance (Shimadzu UV-3600 Plus), FT-IR spectroscopy (Nicolet 6700), and electronic conductivity measurements (JEOL JES-FA200 with EC-RAIR-H operando cell).

First-principles calculations were conducted using the Vienna Ab Initio Simulation Package (VASP) [22] with the projector augmented wave method. The Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) generalized gradient approximation modeled exchange-correlation interactions. A Γ-centered k-mesh with resolutions of 2π × 0.05 Å⁻¹ (geometry optimization) and 2π × 0.02 Å⁻¹ (electronic structure) was employed. Computational parameters included a 450 eV plane-wave cutoff, convergence criteria of 0.05 eV Å⁻¹ for forces and 10⁻⁵ eV for energy, and a 20 Å vacuum layer to mitigate periodic interactions. Van der Waals corrections were applied. A Co0.66Nb1.34O₄ supercell (P42/mnm) served as the base for constructing ten randomized high-entropy doping configurations. After low-precision structural optimization, the lowest-energy configuration was selected for subsequent analysis. Data processing and visualization utilized VASPKIT and VESTA [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis mechanism and functional design of BHS-GQD and HEO-BHS-GQD
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis strategy for BHS-GQD and HEO-BHS-GQD is schematically illustrated in Fig. 1. BHS-GQD is engineered through a multifunctional design: citric acid serves as the carbon precursor for graphene lattice formation, histidine and serine act as functionalizing agents to introduce hydroxyl, carboxyl, and imidazole groups at graphene edges, while boric acid functions as a dopant for boron incorporation. Boron, an electron-deficient element, modulates the semiconductor properties of graphene and synergizes with these functional groups to enhance coordination capacity toward transition metal ions (e.g., Nb5⁺, V5⁺, Mo6⁺, W5⁺, Co2⁺) and improve electrochemical activity.

BHS-GQD was synthesized via a one-step thermal pyrolysis process (175 °C, 6 h) using a precursor mixture with a molar ratio of citric acid/boric acid/histidine/serine = 1:0.2:1:0.2. The product exhibits exceptional water solubility (>2000 g L−1, Fig. S1), attributed to its abundant hydrophilic functional groups.

In the HEO-BHS-GQD composite, BHS-GQD fulfills four critical roles: Chelation: Coordinates with metal ions to form stable, water-soluble complexes, preventing hydrolysis and ensuring atomic-level homogeneity during drying and annealing. Reduction: Reduces high-valent metal species (e.g., Mo6⁺ → Mo4⁺) during thermal treatment, narrowing the HEO bandgap and enhancing electronic conductivity. Morphological Control: Selectively adsorbs onto specific HEO crystal facets via differential binding energies, directing anisotropic growth to form interconnected nanostructures with optimized ion transport channels. Electron Mediation: Carbonizes into conductive graphene sheets at elevated temperatures, establishing efficient electron transfer pathways between HEO nanocrystals and the carbon matrix.

Cotton fibers act as a sacrificial template, spatially isolating metal-BHS-GQD complexes during pyrolysis to prevent aggregation. Carbonization of cotton generates a 3D porous carbon framework that enhances ionic/electronic transport and mitigates mechanical stress during charge/discharge cycles through its high mechanical resilience. This framework also stabilizes the electrode structure by dissipating stress caused by volume changes during cycling.

The HEO composition, (Nb0.32V0.17Mo0.17W0.17Co0.17)2O4, was optimized to leverage super-exchange interactions within Mn+-O2--Co2+ coordination units. These interactions generate a robust built-in electric field, significantly enhancing hybrid capacitive deionization efficiency and enabling cascade redox reactions during electrochemical processes. The selection of specific metal elements is based on their complementary roles: Niobium oxide (Nb2O4): Introduces a shear structure with interconnected tunnels to facilitate rapid ion diffusion. Cobalt (Co2⁺): Stabilizes low-valent states to enable efficient super-exchange interactions via electric field coupling between Mn+ and Co2⁺. Periodic Synergy: Nb, V, Mo, and W (Group VB and VIB elements) exhibit similar electronic structures, promoting electron orbital hybridization and electronic environment reconstruction for enhanced electrochemical activity.

Thermal annealing (850°C, N2 atmosphere, 5°C min⁻¹ heating rate) triggers a gas-mediated explosive crystallization process. Rapid decomposition of BHS-GQD and cotton releases substantial gaseous byproducts (>60 wt% mass loss, Fig. S2), creating localized high-pressure microenvironments that drive intense atomic collisions. This mechanism overcomes the high melting points (>2000 K) of metal precursors, enabling high-entropy alloying despite sub-1000°C annealing temperatures. The homogeneous reddish-brown metal-BHS-GQD complex solution (Fig. S3) infiltrates cotton fibers via capillary action, forming a precursor matrix. Subsequent pyrolysis simultaneously carbonizes the organic components and nucleates HEO nanocrystals, with gas evolution kinetics dictating final crystallite size and distribution.

The pyrolysis temperature is identified as a key determinant of HEO nanocrystal morphology and performance. Experimental results indicate that octahedral HEO nanocrystals form at temperatures exceeding 750°C, with crystallinity improving as temperature rises. However, particle size increases significantly above 950°C, leading to reduced specific capacitance (Fig. S4). To balance crystallinity and particle size, an optimal annealing temperature of 850°C was selected for HEO-BHS-GQD synthesis.

To systematically investigate the role of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) in tailoring the morphology and nanostructure of HEO nanocrystals, three GQD variants-pristine GQD, histidine/serine-functionalized GQD (HS-GQD), and boron-doped HS-GQD (BHS-GQD)-were synthesized and incorporated into HEO composites. Comparative analyses of HEO-GQD, HEO-HS-GQD, and HEO-BHS-GQD were conducted via SEM and XRD. SEM images ( Fig. 2A-C) reveal distinct morphological variations among the composites: HEO-GQD exhibits a cubic morphology with rounded edges and corners. HEO-HS-GQD retains a cubic structure but features sharper edges and well-defined facets. HEO-BHS-GQD adopts a unique "noodle fish-like" morphology characterized by interconnected nanostructures. XRD patterns (Fig. 2D-F) further elucidate structural differences: HEO-GQD displays nineteen diffraction peaks indexed to mixed phases: octahedral (PDF #075–2340, e.g., peaks at 27.1°, 35.4°, and 39.5° corresponding to (110), (101), and (111) planes) and tetrahedral (PDF #0812343, e.g., peaks at 22.9°, 32.6°, 36.1°, 38.1°, 40.7°, 42.1°, 43.3°, 45.9°, 48.7°, 53.4°, 54.4°, 55.3°, 58.1°, 68.2°, 71.3° and 74° assigned to (400), (440), (260), (251), (170), (161), (730), (361), (202), (402), (422), (152), (770), (3110), (1051) and (842) panes), indicating a dual-phase structure. HEO-HS-GQD shows eleven peaks dominated by octahedral symmetry (e.g., 26.9°, 35.4°, 40.1°, 42.6°, 53.1° and 55.5° for (110), (101), (111), (201), (211) and (220) planes) with minor contributions from hexahedral phases (e.g., 31.9°, 36.2°, 44.3°, 46.5° and 72.6° for (440), (501), (730), (800) and (842) planes), reflecting enhanced phase purity compared to HEO-GQD. HEO-BHS-GQD exhibits ten distinct peaks exclusively assigned to octahedral configurations (PDF #075–2340, e.g., 26.7°, 35.3°, 382°, 39.9°, 42.5°, 52.8°, 55.2°, 62.5°, 67.1° and 72.4° for (110), (101), (200), (111), (210), (211), (220), (310), (112) and (320) planes), confirming a single-phase solid solution. Crystallographic analysis (Fig. 2G-H) highlights differences in long- and short-range order: HEO-GQD is dominated by short-range ordered structural units comprising mixed octahedral and tetrahedral nanocrystals. HEO-HS-GQD transitions toward long-range order, with octahedral units becoming predominant. HEO-BHS-GQD achieves exclusive long-range order, where octahedral units interconnect via "head-to-head" tunnel structures. These tunnels facilitate rapid electrolyte penetration during charge/discharge cycles, enhancing electrochemical kinetics.

To understand the effect of GQD on nanostructure of HEO, the electrical conductivity of reaction system after water-soluble Me-GQD complex was formed in the absence of GQD and the presence of different GQDs. Electrical conductivity measurements of precursor solutions (Fig. 2I) reveal critical insights into metal-GQD interactions: No GQD: High conductivity (7.15 μS cm⁻¹) indicates abundant free metal ions in solution. GQD/HS-GQD/BHS-GQD systems: Conductivity decreases progressively (6.22, 5.88, and 3.17 μS cm−1, respectively), demonstrating efficient metal ion coordination by GQDs. The significantly lower conductivity in the BHS-GQD system underscores its superior chelation capacity, ensuring near-complete conversion of metal ions into neutral Me-GQD complexes.

The structural modulation arises from two synergistic mechanisms: Coordination Stability: Strong Me-O/N bonds anchor GQDs onto specific HEO crystal facets (e.g., (110) vs. (101)), selectively inhibiting facet-specific growth rates [24]. Morphological Selectivity: Differential adsorption capacities of GQDs across crystal planes drive anisotropic growth, enabling precise nanostructural engineering. For instance, BHS-GQD’s boron-enhanced electron deficiency strengthens interactions with high-energy facets, favoring the formation of interconnected tunnel structures.

The above study establishes that rational design of GQDs-particularly BHS-GQD-enables precise control over HEO nanocrystal morphology, phase purity, and ion transport pathways. The integration of multifunctional GQDs not only suppresses phase segregation but also stabilizes long-range ordered architectures, offering a scalable strategy to optimize HEO-based electrodes for high-performance energy storage devices.

The structural and compositional features of HEO-BHS-GQD were comprehensively investigated via transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-angle annular dark-field scanning TEM (HAADF-STEM), high-resolution TEM (HRTEM), and elemental mapping, as illustrated in Fig. 3. TEM imaging (Fig. 3A) reveals three distinct components: carbon fibers (light gray, derived from pyrolyzed cotton), graphene sheets (originating from BHS-GQD), and HEO nanocrystals (dark particles). HAADF-STEM (Fig. 3B) further differentiates these components based on atomic mass contrast: carbon fibers appear dark due to their low atomic weight, while HEO nanocrystals (high atomic weight metals) exhibit bright contrast. The unique "noodle fish-like" HEO nanocrystals are uniformly dispersed on carbon fibers, with ultrathin graphene sheets coating their surfaces. HRTEM (Fig. 3C) identifies four distinct regions: Amorphous carbon: Characterized by diffuse (002) ring patterns with a lattice spacing of 0.367 nm (Fig. 3D). Crystalline graphene: Displays sharp (002) diffraction spots (spacing: 0.337 nm, Fig. 3E), confirming its superior crystallinity compared to amorphous carbon. HEO nanocrystals without oxygen vacancies: Exhibit well-defined (110) lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.308 nm (Fig. 3F). HEO nanocrystals with oxygen vacancies: Show expanded (110) lattice spacing (0.321 nm, Fig. 3G), attributed to lattice distortion induced by vacancy formation.

Through the high-resolution images in Fig. 3C and the calibrated fringe spacing in Fig. 3F and G, it can be clearly seen that the synthesized sample is polycrystalline. In the diffraction mode, it should present diffraction rings, but in the picture, it looks more like a lattice. This is due to the local ordering of the crystal structure [25]. Although the sample as a whole is polycrystalline, in analyzing high-resolution images, Fourier transform may only capture signals from selected local regions. If the region happens to contain a larger grain or several grains with similar orientations, the Fourier transform results will show a lattice structure similar to that of a single crystal. This phenomenon is particularly evident in high-resolution images, as local regions of the image may only contain a few grains. In addition, it may also be due to the limitations of Fourier transform. Fourier transform is a global transformation of the selected image region. If certain regions in the selected analysis image region have strong signals (such as diffraction signals of a certain grain), these signals may dominate the Fourier transform results, masking the contributions of other regions and causing the results to appear more like lattices rather than diffraction rings.

Elemental mapping (Fig. 3H-P) confirms the spatial distribution of boron (B, from BHS-GQD), carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and metal constituents (Nb, V, Co, Mo, W) within HEO-BHS-GQD. The homogeneous distribution of all metal elements in HEO nanocrystals verifies the formation of a single-phase high-entropy oxide. Merged elemental maps further demonstrate the absence of phase segregation, consistent with XRD results.

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) in an O₂ atmosphere quantifies the HEO content in the composite as 39.4 wt%, corroborating the successful integration of HEO nanocrystals within the carbon-graphene matrix.

The FTIR spectrum, Raman spectrum of HEO-BHS-GQD, and EPR spectra of HEO and HEO-BHS-GQD are presented in Fig. 4. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 4A) exhibits six distinct IR absorption bands and peaks at wavenumbers of 3421, 2900, 1627, 1434, 1099, and 601 cm−1. The strong IR absorption band observed at approximately 3421 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of O-H, N-H, and =C-H bonds. A weak IR absorption peak at 2900 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the -C-H bond. The strong IR absorption band around 1627 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibrations of CO and CC bonds within the aromatic ring skeleton. The strong IR absorption peak at 1434 cm−1 arises from the stretching vibration of the C-O bond. Additionally, the strong IR absorption peak at 1099 cm−1 is due to the in-plane bending vibration of the O-H bond, while the weak IR absorption peak at 601 cm−1 results from the out-of-plane bending vibration of the C-H bond. These findings collectively indicate that HEO-BHS-GQD contains functional groups such as -NH-, -OH, -COOH, and imidazolyl.

In the Raman spectrum (Fig. 4B), five Raman peaks are observed within the Raman shift range of 100–1900 cm−1. Three weak peaks at Raman shifts of 135, 250, and 809 cm−1 are attributed to Me-O bonds. The two strong Raman peaks at 1320 and 1597 cm−1 correspond to the D and G peaks, respectively, which are characteristic of graphene-based materials.

The EPR spectra of HEO and HEO-BHS-GQD (Fig. 4C) show a notable difference in signal intensity. HEO exhibits a very weak EPR signal, indicating the presence of few oxygen vacancies. In contrast, HEO-BHS-GQD displays a pair of strong EPR peaks at g= 2.002, confirming the existence of abundant oxygen vacancies. This enhancement in oxygen vacancies is attributed to the introduction of BHS-GQD, which facilitates the formation of low-valent metal species. The self-doping of these low-valent metal species subsequently induces the generation of oxygen vacancies in HEO-BHS-GQD.

The XPS spectra of C1s, O1s, N1s, B1s, Nb3d, V2p, Mo3d, W4f, and Co2p for the HEO-BHS-GQD composite are displayed in Fig. 5. The deconvoluted C1s spectrum exhibits seven distinct peaks at binding energies of 284.2, 284.8, 285.2, 285.8, 286.7, 289.3, and 291.4 eV, corresponding to C-B, C-C, C-N, C-O, CO, O-CO, and π-π* interactions, respectively [26]. The O1s spectrum resolves into three components at 530.6, 532.2, and 533.9 eV, attributed to lattice oxygen (Olatt) and adsorption oxygen (Oads) and hydrate oxygen (Ow) [27]. For the N1s spectrum, three peaks are observed at 398.1, 399.6, and 400.2 eV, assigned to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N. The B1s spectrum reveals three contributions at 198.5, 200.2, and 201.9 eV, consistent with B-O bonding environments in B3CO, BC2O, and BCO2 structures [28]. The Nb3d spectrum displays four peaks at 207.4, 208.3, 210.1, and 211.1 eV. The doublet at 207.4 eV (Nb3d5/2) and 210.1 eV (Nb3d3/2) corresponds to Nb4+, while the peaks at 208.3 eV (Nb3d5/2) and 211.1 eV (Nb3d3/2) are assigned to Nb5+ [29]. Similarly, the V2p spectrum shows four peaks at 517.3, 518.1, 524.2, and 526.0 eV. The lower-energy doublet (517.3 eV for V2p3/2 and 524.2 eV for V2p1/2) originates from V4+, whereas the higher-energy pair (518.1 eV for V2p3/2 and 526.0 eV for V2p1/2) arises from V5+ [30]. The Mo3d spectrum contains four peaks at 232.7, 233.3, 235.9, and 237.1 eV. The peaks at 232.7 eV (Mo3d5/2) and 235.9 eV (Mo3d3/2) are attributed to Mo4+, while those at 233.3 eV (Mo3d5/2) and 237.1 eV (Mo3d3/2) correspond to Mo5+ [31]. For W4f, four peaks are observed at 35.7, 36.2, 37.8, and 38.3 eV. The lower-energy doublet (35.7 eV for W4f7/2 and 37.8 eV for W4f5/2) is assigned to W5+, while the higher-energy pair (36.2 eV for W4f7/2 and 38.3 eV for W4f5/2) corresponds to W6+ [32]. The Co2p spectrum exhibits six peaks at 781.9, 784.5, 787.9, 797.1, 799.4, and 803.9 eV. The primary spin-orbit doublet at 781.9 eV (Co2p3/2) and 797.1 eV (Co2p1/2) arises from Co³ ⁺, while the pair at 784.5 eV (Co2p3/2) and 799.4 eV (Co2p1/2) is associated with Co2+. The remaining peaks at 787.9 and 803.9 eV are attributed to satellite features (Sat) [6]. Collectively, these results confirm the coexistence of low-valent Nb4+, V4+, Mo4+, W6+, and Co2+ species within the HEO-BHS-GQD framework. The self-doping effect induced by these mixed valence states facilitates the generation of oxygen vacancies, which effectively narrow the material’s bandgap and enhance its electrical conductivity.

The chronoamperometric and cyclic voltammetry (CV) behaviors of the HEO-BHS-GQD electrode in a 1 M Li2SO4 aqueous electrolyte were systematically investigated to assess the influence of H2 and O₂ evolution during charge-discharge processes. As shown in Fig. 6A, chronoamperometric curves recorded at varying applied voltages reveal distinct trends. At voltages below −1.0 V, the current density remains below 0.1 A g−1, indicating negligible hydrogen evolution under these conditions. However, a pronounced increase in current density (>0.1 A g−1) is observed when the voltage exceeds −1.0 V, signifying substantial interference from H₂ evolution. Consequently, a lower voltage limit of −1.0 V was selected to minimize parasitic reactions. Similarly, Fig. 6B presents CV curves measured across different voltage ranges. At voltages below 0.8 V, the CV profiles exhibit stable behavior at the high-voltage terminus, suggesting minimal oxygen evolution. In contrast, a sharp current surge emerges at voltages above 0.8 V, confirming significant O₂ evolution interference. Thus, an upper voltage limit of 0.8 V was adopted. By integrating these findings, a stable electrochemical window of 1.8 V (-1.0–0.8 V) was established for subsequent measurements to mitigate gas evolution effects.

Fig. 6C compares the CV profiles of HEO-BHS-GQD and pristine HEO electrodes within the optimized voltage window. The HEO-BHS-GQD electrode demonstrates a broader operational window and markedly higher current density, underscoring the beneficial role of BHS-GQD incorporation. This enhancement arises from two synergistic effects: (1) BHS-GQD promotes the formation of smaller HEO nanocrystals with abundant electrochemically active sites, and (2) it introduces defect energy levels and interconnected conductive pathways, significantly improving charge transfer kinetics. Fig. 6D further corroborates these conclusions, as the CV current density escalates sharply with increasing scan rates, reflecting the superior conductivity of the BHS-GQD-modified electrode.

Galvanostatic charge-discharge profiles and specific capacitance values for HEO, HEO-GQD, HEO-HS-GQD, and HEO-BHS-GQD electrodes at 1 A g⁻¹ are presented in Fig. 6E and F. All discharge curves exhibit a two-stage voltage decay: a gradual decline associated with electric double-layer capacitance (EDLC) and a rapid drop linked to pseudocapacitance. The specific capacitances follow the order HEO-BHS-GQD (966.0 F g−1) > HEO-HS-GQD (545.4 F g−1) > HEO-GQD (468.0 F g−1) > HEO (288.8 F g−1). The poor performance of pristine HEO stems from its semiconductor-like low conductivity, which severely restricts charge transfer kinetics and limits redox reaction completion during discharge.

Notably, HEO-GQD exhibits a twofold capacitance increase over HEO, attributed to graphene quantum dot (GQD)-induced structural modifications. GQD incorporation facilitates (1) downsizing of HEO nanocrystals with interconnected ion diffusion channels, (2) graphene surface passivation to enhance electron transport, and (3) generation of oxygen vacancies via reduction of high-valent metal species during annealing. These combined effects amplify active site accessibility and conductivity. However, HEO-BHS-GQD outperforms all variants, achieving a specific capacitance of 966.0 F g−1. Remarkably, when normalized to the HEO mass fraction within the composite, the HEO component alone exhibits an extraordinary capacitance of 2415.7 F g−1 over eightfold higher than pristine HEO. This breakthrough highlights the critical role of BHS-GQD in optimizing both nanostructural architecture and electronic properties, ultimately enabling unprecedented energy storage performance.

The galvanostatic charge-discharge profiles of the HEO-BHS-GQD electrode at varying current densities in 1 M Li2SO4 aqueous electrolyte, along with the specific capacitance versus current density relationships for all tested electrodes, are presented in Fig. 6G and H. A consistent trend is observed across all systems: specific capacitance diminishes with escalating current density due to insufficient time for complete electrochemical reactions during rapid discharge processes. Pristine HEO exhibits the poorest rate capability, with its capacitance plummeting from 288.8 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 to 87.5 F g−1 at 50 A g−1.

Notably, GQD-functionalized electrodes demonstrate markedly enhanced rate performance. For instance, HEO-GQD retains 326.3 F g−1 (69.7 % of its initial value) at 10 A g−1, significantly outperforming unmodified HEO. Crucially, the structural configuration of GQD derivatives profoundly impacts high-rate behavior. Among the series, HEO-BHS-GQD emerges as the superior candidate, delivering a remarkable 966.0 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 while maintaining 636.3 F g−1 (65.9 % retention) at 10 A g−1 and 532.9 F g−1 (55.2 % retention) at 50 A g−1. This exceptional performance hierarchy-HEO-BHS-GQD > HEO-HS-GQD > HEO-GQD > HEO-underscores the critical role of BHS-GQD in constructing robust conductive networks and optimizing charge transfer kinetics. The results conclusively establish BHS-GQD as the optimal graphene quantum dot variant for engineering high-performance HEO nanocrystals with superior energy storage capabilities under demanding operational conditions.

The electrochemical performance of the HEO-BHS-GQD electrode was systematically investigated in four aqueous electrolytes (1 M KOH, 1 M NaCl, 1 M H2SO4, and 1 M Li2SO4) by analyzing cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves at 5 mV s−1, galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) profiles, and specific capacitances at 1 A g−1. As illustrated in Fig. S5A, the Li2SO4 electrolyte enabled the widest operational voltage window (1.8 V, −1.0–0.8 V), surpassing those observed in H₂SO₄ (1.2 V, −0.3–0.9 V), KOH (1.5 V, −0.9–0.6 V), and NaCl (1.1 V, −0.2–0.9 V). This enhanced voltage window is attributed to the superior chemical inertness of Li2SO4, which mitigates parasitic side reactions and stabilizes the electrode-electrolyte interface. Although the GCD curves across electrolytes exhibited similar triangular symmetry (Fig. S5B), indicating consistent capacitive energy storage mechanisms, significant discrepancies emerged in specific capacitance values. The Li2SO4 electrolyte delivered the highest specific capacitance of 966.0 F g⁻¹ , substantially exceeding those of H₂SO₄ (704 F g−1), NaCl (638 F g−1), and KOH (441 F g−1) (Fig. S5C). This performance enhancement is ascribed to two synergistic factors: (i) the expanded voltage window, which increases charge accumulation capacity, and (ii) the accelerated ion diffusion kinetics facilitated by the smaller hydrated ionic radius of Li(H2O)6 + (2.1 Å) compared to K(H2O)8 + (2.8 Å), Na(H2O)6 + (2.4 Å), and H⁺ (hydronium dynamics, 2.5–3 Å) [33], as supported by prior studies on cation mobility in aqueous systems. These findings underscore the critical role of electrolyte selection in optimizing supercapacitor performance through tailored ion transport and interfacial stability.

To evaluate the supercapacitor performance of HEO-BHS-GQD, a symmetrical supercapacitor was constructed using HEO-BHS-GQD as the electrodes and PVA/Li2SO4 as the gel electrolyte. Fig. 7A illustrates the CV curves of the supercapacitor at different voltage ranges. The CV curve exhibits a flat profile at the high-voltage end when the voltage range is below 1.8 V, indicating negligible O2 evolution. However, a pronounced surge emerges in the CV curve at higher voltages, demonstrating that O2 evolution significantly impacts electrochemical measurements. Based on these observations, a safe voltage window of 1.8 V was selected for further analysis. Within this range, CV curves at varying scan rates are displayed in Fig. 7B. Distinct protrusions observed in the curves are ascribed to the oxidation and reduction of metal species in HEO-BHS-GQD. As the scan rate increases, the CV current rises sharply, confirming the high conductivity of HEO-BHS-GQD.

Fig. 7C and D present the galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) curves at different current densities and the corresponding specific capacitance versus current density plot. The specific capacitance decreases with increasing current density due to the faster discharge process relative to the electrode reaction kinetics. This mismatch limits the time available for complete redox reactions of all metal species in the HEO-BHS-GQD electrode, resulting in reduced capacitance. Notably, the specific capacitance reaches 340 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, demonstrating exceptional capacitance retention. Even at 10 A g−1 and 50 A g−1, the values remain high at 225.9 F g−1 and 161.3 F g−1, respectively, highlighting excellent rate capability.

Fig. 7E depicts the Ragone plot (energy density vs. power density) for the symmetrical supercapacitor. The device achieves an energy density of 153 Wh kg−1 at 900 W kg−1, comparable to lithium-ion batteries. Remarkably, it retains 102 Wh kg−1 at 8571 W kg−1 and 73 Wh kg−1 at 45,000 W kg−1, underscoring its suitability for high-power applications.

Cycling stability was evaluated over 10,000 cycles at 10 A g−1 (Fig. 7F). The initial specific capacitance of 225.0 F g−1 slightly declines to 220.5 F g−1 after cycling, corresponding to a retention rate of 98.2 %. This minimal degradation confirms outstanding long-term stability.

To assess practical applicability, supercapacitors were connected in series or parallel configurations. In series, the voltage doubles to 3.6 V while maintaining similar charge-discharge times to a single unit (Fig. 7G). Parallel configurations enhance output current, enabling tailored power delivery for diverse scenarios-series connections for high-voltage devices and parallel arrangements for high-current demands.

Flexibility testing under mechanical deformation (Fig. 7H) reveals only minor capacitance loss with increasing folding/twisting angles, confirming robust electrochemical durability and suitability for wearable electronics.

To investigate the electrochemically active components responsible for energy storage in the high-entropy oxide-biochar-holey graphene quantum dot (HEO-BHS-GQD) composite, ex situ X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted on the electrode material under pristine, fully charged, and fully discharged states. As illustrated in Fig. 8A, the overall XPS spectra across these three states exhibited negligible differences, with no emergence or disappearance of characteristic peaks. This observation confirms that the electrochemical energy storage mechanism occurs within the bulk structure of HEO-BHS-GQD rather than through surface adsorption or irreversible phase transitions (Fig. 8B). However, valence state transformations of constituent elements were detected during charge-discharge cycling, corresponding to electron transfer processes (Fig. 8C-I and Table S1).

High-resolution C1s spectra revealed that carbon species in the pristine state comprised C-B, C-C, C-O, CO, O-CO, π-π* interactions, and transient Li-C bonds (Fig. 8C) [26]. Notably, the Li-C bond intensity increased during charging but diminished upon discharge, suggesting reversible Li⁺ adsorption/desorption at the electrode-electrolyte interface. Given the minimal valence variation of carbon throughout cycling, carbon components were excluded as contributors to pseudocapacitance. Similarly, oxygen species (Li-O, lattice oxygen [Olatt], adsorbed oxygen [Oads], and water-associated oxygen [Ow]) displayed reversible proportion changes without valence state interconversion, ruling out oxygen's involvement in pseudocapacitance generation (Fig. 8D).

In contrast, transition metal elements exhibited pronounced redox activity. Charging induced a reduction in low-valence species (Nb4+, V4+, Mo4+, W5+, Co3+) alongside an increase in high-valence counterparts (Nb4.5+/Nb5+, V5+, Mo5+/Mo6+, W6+, Co2+), indicative of electron acquisition (reduction) (Fig. 8E-I). Conversely, discharging triggered oxidation processes, with low-valence species (Nb4+, V2+/V3+/V4+, Mo4+, W5+, Co3+) regenerated at the expense of high-valence states. These systematic valence transitions confirm that Nb, V, Mo, W, and Co serve as the primary electrochemically active centers, driving pseudocapacitance through reversible redox reactions.

This work elucidates the element-specific charge storage mechanisms in HEO-BHS-GQD, providing critical insights for designing high-performance energy storage materials through tailored multi-element synergy.

To elucidate the impact of valence-state transitions in metallic species on the structural integrity of high-entropy oxide (HEO) nanocrystals, ex situ X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed on the HEO-BHS-GQD electrode under pristine, fully charged, and fully discharged conditions. As shown in Fig. 9A, the XRD patterns across these states remained largely consistent, indicating that redox-induced valence changes in metal species did not compromise the structural stability of HEO nanocrystals. This remarkable resilience is attributed to the synergistic effects of interconnected structural tunnels and a robust three-dimensional carbon framework, which effectively dissipate mechanical stresses generated during electrochemical cycling.

A detailed examination of the (110) lattice plane revealed dynamic structural adjustments during charge-discharge processes. Upon charging, the diffraction peak shifted from 26.97° (pristine) to 27.12°, signifying contraction of the interplanar spacing (Fig. 9B). Conversely, discharging induced a reverse shift to 26.99°, corresponding to lattice expansion. These reversible changes directly correlate with Li⁺ intercalation/deintercalation events, where Li⁺ insertion enhances interlayer electrostatic attraction, thereby reducing lattice dimensions, while extraction relaxes the structure. Such behavior not only confirms Li⁺ participation in the charge storage mechanism but also underscores the generation of intercalation pseudocapacitance.

Notably, each Li⁺ intercalation event facilitates electron storage within the HEO lattice, with the material’s high surface area and hierarchical porosity enabling rapid ion diffusion to redox-active sites (Nb, V, Mo, W, Co). This promotes efficient redox kinetics without inducing irreversible crystallographic phase transitions or structural deformation, as evidenced by the preserved XRD patterns. The absence of phase degradation highlights the critical role of HEO-BHS-GQD’s architectural design in balancing high-capacity redox activity with long-term cyclability.

Collectively, these findings establish that the HEO-BHS-GQD system achieves exceptional structural stability through a synergistic combination of dynamic lattice adaptability, conductive carbon networks, and multi-element redox activity. This work advances the rational design of high-entropy materials for durable, high-performance energy storage applications.

To elucidate the charge storage mechanism of the high-entropy oxide-biochar-holey graphene quantum dot (HEO-BHS-GQD) composite, cyclic voltammetry (CV) was conducted at scan rates ranging from 5 to 40 mV s⁻¹ in a 1.0 M Li2SO4 electrolyte ( Fig. 10A). The CV profiles exhibit multiple redox peaks within a stable potential window of 1.8 V (-1.0–0.8 V), corresponding to the oxidation and reduction of Nb, V, Mo, W, and Co species in the HEO-BHS-GQD matrix. Enhanced current densities at higher scan rates reflect favorable electrode kinetics, attributed to the composite’s high electrical conductivity and efficient charge transfer pathways.

The charge storage dynamics were further analyzed using the power-law relationship: [34] i = av
b (1) where b-values of 0.75 (cathodic) and 0.73 (anodic) were derived from the linear regression of log(i) vs. log(v) plots (Fig. 10B). These intermediate bb-values (0.5 < b < 1.0) suggest a hybrid charge storage mechanism involving both diffusion-controlled redox processes and surface-dominated capacitive contributions. Quantitative deconvolution of these contributions was performed using the equation:

i = k 1 v+k 2 v+k 3 v 1/2 (2) where k 1, k2, and k 3 represent the contributions of electric double-layer capacitance (EDLC), surface redox pseudocapacitance, and diffusion-controlled bulk redox capacitance, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 10C, diffusion-controlled redox processes dominate at low scan rates (61.9 % at 5 mV s−1), underscoring the pivotal role of Li⁺ intercalation into the HEO-BHS-GQD lattice. However, the redox contribution diminishes with increasing scan rates due to insufficient time for Li⁺ diffusion into the interconnected tunnels and subsequent bulk redox reactions. Conversely, EDLC and surface redox contributions rise at higher scan rates, facilitated by the composite’s three-dimensional carbon framework, which enables rapid interfacial electron/ion transport. Remarkably, diffusion-controlled capacitance (175.7 F g−1 at 5 mV s−1) consistently surpasses surface contributions by factors of 2.5 (surface redox) and 5 (EDLC) across all tested rates (Fig. 10D).

These findings conclusively establish that the exceptional specific capacitance of HEO-BHS-GQD originates predominantly from diffusion-controlled redox reactions, while the hierarchical porous architecture ensures sustained high-rate performance by balancing bulk and interfacial charge storage processes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of BHS-GQD on the energy storage
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the impact of biochar-holey graphene quantum dots (BHS-GQD) on the electrical conductivity of high-entropy oxide (HEO) nanocrystals, the semiconductor properties of HEO, HEO-GQD, HEO-HS-GQD, and HEO-BHS-GQD were systematically analyzed via UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS). As shown in Fig. 11A, pristine HEO exhibits strong ultraviolet light absorption but negligible visible light absorption. The integration of GQD significantly enhances visible light absorption, with further improvements observed upon replacing GQD with holey sulfur-doped GQD (HS-GQD) and BHS-GQD. Notably, HEO-BHS-GQD demonstrates the highest visible light absorption capacity, suggesting optimized electronic interactions within the composite.

Bandgap energy (E g) values, derived from Kubelka-Munk transformed spectra ( Fig. 11B), reveal a progressive reduction from 2.75 eV (HEO) to 0.6 eV (HEO-BHS-GQD). This trend correlates with the introduction of defect states via low-valent metal species formed during annealing, which narrow the bandgap and enhance charge carrier mobility. The hierarchical structure of BHS-GQD, characterized by abundant edge sites and porous frameworks, further amplifies this effect, yielding the lowest E g and highest electrical conductivity among the tested materials.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in 1 M Li2SO4 electrolyte corroborates these findings (Fig. 11C, D and Table S2). Charge transfer resistance (R
ct ) decreases dramatically from 268.5 Ω (HEO) to 5.96 Ω (HEO-BHS-GQD), reflecting a 45-fold enhancement in conductivity. Intermediate composites (HEO-GQD: 38.76 Ω; HEO-HS-GQD: 19.48 Ω) confirm that structural modifications in GQD derivatives-such as N doping and biochar integration-critically modulate interfacial charge transfer kinetics. The reduced R
ct aligns with the diminished E
g , underscoring the synergistic role of defect engineering and conductive carbon networks in optimizing HEO’s electronic properties.

This work establishes that tailoring GQD architectures (e.g., porosity, heteroatom doping) effectively tunes the semiconductor behavior of HEO composites, offering a versatile strategy for designing high-conductivity materials for energy storage and conversion applications.

To evaluate the effect of BHS-GQD on the ion diffusion kinetics, the relationship between the imaginary impedance component (Z″) and the square root of frequency (f¹/²) was analyzed for HEO, HEO-GQD, HEO-HS-GQD, and HEO-BHS-GQD electrodes (Fig. 11E), derived from the Nyquist plots in Fig. 11C and D. A strong linear correlation was observed between Z″ and f¹ /² for all samples. The slopes (σw) of these linear fits were utilized to calculate the lithium-ion diffusion coefficients (DLi+) using established equations [35]. The calculated DLi+ values increased significantly across the series: 4.75 × 10−12 cm2 s−1 for HEO, 1.68 × 10−10 cm2 s−1 for HEO-GQD, 6.01 × 10−10 cm2 s−1 for HEO-HS-GQD, and 8.52 × 10−9 cm2 s−1 for HEO-BHS-GQD. These results highlight the critical role of GQD incorporation in enhancing Li⁺ diffusion, with the BHS-GQD modification inducing a remarkable 1793-fold improvement in DLi+ compared to the pristine HEO nanocrystal.

The electrochemical surface area (ECSA) of the electrodes was further investigated through cyclic voltammetry (CV) in 0.1 M KNO₃ electrolyte. As shown in Fig. 12A, the HEO electrode exhibited the lowest current density, indicative of minimal electrochemically active sites. In contrast, electrodes modified with GQD derivatives (HEO-GQD, HEO-H-GQD, HEO-HS-GQD, and HEO-BHS-GQD) demonstrated progressively enhanced current densities (Fig. 12B-D), confirming that the integration of functionalized GQDs substantially increases accessible active sites. Notably, the HEO-BHS-GQD electrode displayed the highest CV current density, reflecting optimal ECSA.

Quantitative analysis of the ECSA was performed by correlating the current density variation (ΔJ) with scan rates (Fig. 12E). Linear regression of these plots yielded slope values (s) of 0.012 for HEO-BHS-GQD, 0.0073 for HEO-HS-GQD, 0.006 for HEO-H-GQD, 0.0048 for HEO-GQD, and 0.0001 for HEO. Given that the slope directly correlates with ECSA for analogous nanomaterials [36], these data reveal a 120-fold enhancement in ECSA for the HEO-BHS-GQD composite relative to the unmodified HEO. Collectively, these findings underscore the synergistic effects of BHS-GQD functionalization in simultaneously improving ion diffusion kinetics and expanding electrochemically active interfaces.

To elucidate the mechanism underlying the enhanced supercapacitor performance of HEO-BHS-GQD composites, density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed to analyze electronic structure and interfacial charge dynamics. Fig. 13A presents the total density of states (TDOS) plots for HEO-GQD, HEO-HS-GQD, and HEO-BHS-GQD. All HEO-based materials exhibit broad TDOS features spanning from the valence band edge near −3 eV to the conduction band edge at + 3 eV, indicative of metallic-like conductivity. This continuous electronic state distribution facilitates rapid electron transport, attributed to the synergistic hybridization of d-orbitals from Nb, V, Mo, W, and Co within the high-entropy oxide matrix (Fig. S6). Notably, substituting GQD with HS-GQD or BHS-GQD significantly amplifies the TDOS intensity between −3 and + 3 eV (Fig. 13A). This enhancement arises from the hybridization of 2p orbitals of N and B atoms in BHS-GQD with the d-orbitals of transition metals (Fig. S7), confirming that histidine (His), serine (Ser), and boron functionalization collectively optimize the electronic conductivity of HEO-BHS-GQD.

Interfacial charge redistribution was further investigated via charge density difference maps (Fig. 13B-D). For HEO-GQD, minimal charge accumulation/depletion is observed at the heterojunction (Fig. 13B). In contrast, introducing N and B heteroatoms in HEO-HS-GQD and HEO-BHS-GQD induces pronounced charge polarization at the interface (Fig. 13C-D). The HEO-BHS-GQD composite exhibits the most extensive charge redistribution, generating a robust built-in electric field at the heterointerface. This field accelerates interfacial electron transfer and enhances hybrid capacitive deionization kinetics.

In summary, BHS-GQD functionalization synergistically enhances three critical parameters: electrical conductivity (45-fold increase), Li⁺ diffusion coefficient (1793-fold improvement), and electrochemically active sites (120-fold expansion). These interconnected enhancements-stemming from optimized electronic structure, intensified interfacial charge dynamics, and enlarged surface reactivity-collectively drive the exceptional supercapacitor performance of HEO-BHS-GQD, positioning it as a promising candidate for advanced energy storage systems.

To elucidate the role of superexchange interactions in enhancing hybrid capacitive deionization (HCDI), a schematic model was constructed based on XPS analysis ( Fig. 14). The HEO-BHS-GQD architecture comprises nine distinct Mn+-O2--Co2+ coordination units, where Mn+ represents a heterogeneous ensemble of transition metal cations, including Nb5+/4+, V5+/4+, Mo5+/4+, W6+/5+, and Co3+. In these units, oxygen anions (O2-) mediate superexchange coupling between Mn+ and Co2+ through spin-polarized electron transfer under an applied electric field.

According to Hund's multiplicity rule, one O2p electron with parallel spin occupies the t2 g or eg orbital of Mn+, while a second electron with antiparallel spin is transferred to the degenerate eg orbital of Co2+. This spin-correlated charge transfer induces pronounced charge separation, generating a robust internal electric field that synergistically enhances electron transport kinetics and ion diffusion across the HEO-BHS-GQD matrix. Crucially, the cooperative interplay of nine distinct Mn+-O2−-Co2+ units amplifies the superexchange-driven polarization effect, significantly improving the charge storage capacity and desalination efficiency of the hybrid capacitive deionization (HCDI) system. Such multi-cationic synergy provides a novel pathway for optimizing interfacial charge dynamics in heterogeneous electrocatalytic materials.

The electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra (Fig. S8) provide compelling evidence for robust electron-electron coupling within the ultralong electron transport chain. Key spectral parameters, including the half-height width (ΔH) and integral area (SIA) derived from first-order integration curves, were systematically analyzed. The energy difference (ΔE) of reverse spintronic transitions was calculated from ΔH values in Table S3. Notably, the SIA of (Nb₀.₃₇V₀.₂₁Mo₀.₂₁W₀.₂₁)₂O₄-BHS-GQD is significantly smaller than that of its Co-doped counterpart (Nb₀.₃₂V₀.₁₇Mo₀.₁₇W₀.₁₇Co₀.₁₇)₂O₄-BHS-GQD, demonstrating that low-valent Co doping enhances oxygen vacancy concentration in high-entropy oxides [37]. This conclusion aligns with prior studies where ΔH and ΔE directly correlate with the coupling intensity between unpaired electrons of adjacent cations [38].

Intriguingly, Co doping elevates both ΔH and ΔE, amplifying intermetallic coupling strength. Concurrently, oxygen vacancies facilitate enhanced orbital overlap between neighboring cations. The synergistic interplay of these effects-stronger metal-metal coupling and vacancy-mediated electronic interactions-creates an optimal environment for reverse spintronic transitions. Such cooperative mechanisms are critical for accelerating electron transfer kinetics in the (Nb0.32V0.17Mo0.17W0.17Co0.17)2O4-BHS-GQD system, as evidenced by its superior electron transport performance. These findings underscore the pivotal role of defect engineering and cation synergy in tailoring electronic coupling dynamics for advanced spintronic applications.

To systematically probe the influence of the internal electric field on the HCDI, electrochemical evaluations were conducted in a 500 mg L−1 NaCl solution under a ± 1.2 V potential window at a flow rate of 30 mL min−1 (batch mode). As depicted in Fig. 15A, the rapid decline in solution conductivity during the 1.2 V adsorption phase and its swift recovery at −1.2 V (desorption) highlight the reversible Na+ capture-release dynamics. Notably, the HEO-BHS-GQD electrode exhibited a conductivity change of 131.5 μS cm−1, reflecting superior ion transport kinetics. This performance enhancement correlates directly with the increased compositional complexity of HEO, underscoring the critical role of multi-metallic synergy in accelerating desalination.

The salt removal capacity (SRC) of HEO-BHS-GQD reached 41.8 mg g−1, surpassing control electrodes such as Nb2O5-BHS-GQD (25.1 mg g−1), (Nb0.65V0.35)2O4-BHS-GQD (29.1 mg g−1) and ternary/quaternary oxide hybrids (33.3–34.9 mg g−1) (Fig. 15B). Concurrently, the salt removal rate (SRR) peaked at 7.2 mg g−1 min−1 for HEO-BHS-GQD, outperforming counterparts by 18–41 % (Fig. 15C). Current decay profiles (Fig. 15D) further validated the electrolyte depletion mechanism, while a charge efficiency of 61.9 % for HEO-BHS-GQD (vs. 49.6–58.3 % for others, Fig. 15E) confirmed minimized energy loss. The Kim-Yoon plot positioned HEO-BHS-GQD in the upper-right quadrant, signifying optimal HCDI behavior.

These exceptional metrics stem from the robust internal electric field generated by superexchange interactions within the Mn+-O2--Co2+ coordination units. This field facilitates concerted electron-ion transport, reducing interfacial resistance and enhancing charge storage capacity. The multi-cationic HEO architecture thus establishes a paradigm for designing high-performance desalination materials through atomic-level electronic structure engineering.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison with reported electrode materials for supercapacitors
ce:section.ce:para: Table 1 compares the supercapacitor performance of HEO-BHS-GQD with other high-entropy metal oxide-based electrodes. HEO-BHS-GQD outperforms all listed materials, establishing its superiority in energy storage applications. Such an excellent supercapacitor performance should be attributed to three reasons in the following:

Firstly, we use strong coordination of BHS-GQD with metal ions to realize a uniform distribution of all metal elements at atom level in a solution state and in a solid state. This results in formation of long-range orderly HEO nanocrystals with interconnected tunnels. The finding provides us one new insight for synthesis of high-entropy oxide nanoparticles with single-phase structure by introducing the graphene quantum dot with strong chelating ability towards metal ions.

Secondly, we use the introduction of BHS-GQD to realize more than 45-fold increase in electrical conductivity, 1793-fold increase in Li+ diffusion coefficient and 120-fold increase in electrochemical active sites. The integration of these factors leads to a significantly improved supercapacitor performance. In addition, the study also reveals morphology, nanostructure and electrochemical properties seriously depend on the structure of graphene quantum dot. The finding offers us one new insight for controlling the structure and improving supercapacitor performance of high-entropy oxide nanoparticles by rational design of graphene quantum dot.

Thirdly, we use the introduction of super-exchange interaction to realize highly efficient hybrid capacitive deionization. The super-exchange interaction on Mn+ -O2--Co2 + coordination structure units induce the production of strong internal electric field in HEO-BHS-GQD. The presence of strong built-in electric field promotes the electron and ion transfer and leads to an improved supercapacitor performance. The finding offers use one new insight for improving supercapacitor performance of high-entropy oxide nanoparticles by rational design coordination structure units. In particular, special electronic structure of Co element is exploited.

An advanced (Nb0.32V0.17Mo0.17W0.17Co0.17)2O4 high entropy oxide nanocrystal (HEO) electrode material has been successfully constructed by introducing multifunctional BHS-GQD and super-exchange interaction. The nanostructure and electrochemical property of HEO-based electrode materials depends on the structure of graphene quantum dot. The strong coordination of BHS-GQD with metal ions firmly suppress the structural homogeneity in high-entropy oxide and leads to the formation of long range ordered HEO nanocrystals with interconnected tunnels. The introduction of BHS-GQD also brings a significant improvement in the electrical conductivity, ion diffusion efficiency and electrochemically active area. The introduction of Mn+-O2--Co2+ coordination structure unit accelerates the hybrid capacitive deionization. As a result, HEO-BHS-GQD electrode exhibits outstanding supercapacitor performance, including high specific capacitance, high-rate capacity, cycling stability. More importantly, the study offers one new insight for improvement of structural homogeneity and conductivity in high entropy oxide nanoparticles in energy storage and conversion, catalysis and biosensing by rational design of graphene quantum dot and super-exchange interaction structure.

Xiaohao Liu: Investigation. Li Zaijun: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yirui Shen: Methodology, Investigation. Ruiyi Li: Investigation. Xiaoshan Li: Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis.
